claim: Fine-tuning a feature extractor for question classification, then using prompt routing (different prompts/tools based on intent) is more effective than model routing, because it scales when the base model improves and doesn't require constant upkeep against frontier models.
actionable: Build a fine-tuned classifier to extract intent/subject from inputs, then route to hand-crafted prompts and workflows instead of delegating routing to the LLM.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: OCR the image, then pass to a fine-tuned model that extracts subject/subtopic, enabling better routing of the response format (e.g., step-by-step for math vs. narrative for history).
actionable: For visual problem-solving apps: OCR the image, classify the subject/subtopic with a fine-tuned model, then deliver the response format that matches the question type.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Use feature flags (LaunchDarkly) to route traffic across multiple regional API endpoints instead of building a traditional load balancer; scales cost-effectively and allows dynamic rebalancing without redeployment.
actionable: Replace traditional load balancing with feature flag routing: each request hits the flag and randomly routes to one of multiple regional API endpoints.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Run a de-indexer every few days to remove unused embeddings from the vector index (keeping data but not index entries), then reload on-demand; avoids hitting storage tier limits in expensive vector databases like Azure AI Search.
actionable: Implement a background job that removes embeddings unused for N days from the index, then lazy-load them when users request them again.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Release a pure vector search beta to users, observe what they try to do with it, categorize the intents, then build deterministic LLM-driven workflows for each intent (not open-ended agent tools). This gives you debugging clarity, reliable behavior, and the ability to tune specific intents.
actionable: When building an agent: start with simple retrieval, collect user attempts, extract intents, then replace with predetermined but LLM-parameterized workflows for each intent.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Claude is significantly better than OpenAI at generating human-sounding, conversational text that resonates with Gen Z; OpenAI tends to add fake-sounding hashtags and emojis.
actionable: For consumer-facing text generation requiring natural tone and demographic resonance (Gen Z, conversational): prefer Claude over OpenAI.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Viral growth on TikTok is partially deterministic: systematize the anatomy of successful videos (angle, content backdrop, handwriting clarity, UGC style), then repeat the formula across multiple videos to compound reach.
actionable: Analyze your viral hits for common elements (framing, backdrop, visual clarity); codify the formula and execute multiple variations to compound viral reach.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Modern consumer software behaves like CPG (consumer packaged goods) rather than traditional tech: distribution, branding, and lifestyle alignment matter more than feature parity; users are more sophisticated about subscription value.
actionable: For consumer products: invest in distribution, brand voice, and community alignment as your primary moat, not just product features.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Split the org into product teams (owning individual apps, metrics, revenue, churn) and a platform team (owning shared architecture, AI orchestration, notification systems, etc.). Product teams focus on UX/revenue; platform scales deterministic workflows.
actionable: Restructure: product engineers own individual product P&Ls and user experience; platform engineers own shared systems and agentic workflows.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: As LLMs improve, prompt engineering ROI drops. Focus instead on workflow orchestration: how do you build determinism into a non-deterministic system via constraints, routing, and parameterization?
actionable: Shift engineering effort from tweaking prompts to designing deterministic workflows with clear intent routing and parameterized LLM calls.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve

claim: Build regular automation and dashboards first to see if they solve the problem; only then build agents if you need to parallelize and scale the workflow.
actionable: For any workflow you want to automate: start with rules-based automation or better tooling; graduate to agents only if you need to scale horizontally.
source_title: Tiny Teams: $6m ARR, 5m users with 4 employees — Sid Bendre, Oleve
